title: Oxford Entrance Test
seoDescription: Take a fast Oxford-style reasoning quiz with 80 staged questions covering logic, patterns, evidence, numbers, and interview-style thinking.
pageTitle: Only 3% Can Pass This Oxford Test
summary: Only 3% can pass this elite reasoning test.

homepage.title: Can You Pass This Oxford Entrance Test?
homepage.summary: Only 3% can pass this elite reasoning test.

infoPanel.title: About This Oxford Test
infoPanel.intro: This experience is inspired by the kind of logical thinking, pattern recognition, and problem-solving often associated with competitive Oxford-style admissions discussion. It is designed for entertainment, curiosity, and self-challenge, not as an official University of Oxford examination.

columns.title: How It Works
columns.body: The quiz unfolds across multiple stages. Early rounds focus on quick observations and accessible logic, while later stages introduce more layered reasoning, shifting patterns, evidence judgement, and unfamiliar problems designed to test flexible thinking.

columns.title: What This Quiz Tests
columns.body: Rather than copying real admissions papers, this challenge explores broader reasoning skills often linked with high-level academic problem solving.

infoPanel.footerBody: Every question includes one correct answer and a short explanation. Scores are intended as a fun benchmark for self-challenge and comparison, not as a real academic assessment.

landing.challengeText: It starts easy, but the final rounds test serious reasoning.

profiles.title: You thought like a top applicant.
profiles.copy: Exceptional reasoning across the full challenge. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Strong performance.
profiles.copy: You stayed sharp as the questions became more demanding. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Good score.
profiles.copy: You handled the core reasoning rounds well. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Keep sharpening.
profiles.copy: The later rounds reward calm pattern recognition and careful logic. Your best area was {stage}.

stageGroups.title: Quick-Fire Warm Up
stageGroups.encouragement: Good start. The first round was about fast, clear thinking. Next, the patterns get a little sharper.

questions.prompt: Which item does not belong with the others?
questions.explanation: A pillow is used for sleeping, while the others are common study items.

questions.prompt: If 3 + 2 = 5, what is 6 + 3?
questions.explanation: 6 plus 3 equals 9.

questions.prompt: Which word best completes the idea: key is to lock as password is to ____?
questions.explanation: A key allows access to a lock; a password allows access to a system.

questions.prompt: A student has five notes and files two away. How many notes remain on the desk?
questions.explanation: Starting with 5 and filing away 2 leaves 3.

questions.prompt: Which shape has four equal sides?
questions.explanation: A square has four equal sides.

questions.prompt: Which statement is most clearly true?
choices: A line has thickness by definition
questions.explanation: A square has four sides; the other statements are not generally true.

questions.prompt: Which number comes next: 1, 3, 5, 7, ____?
questions.explanation: The sequence increases by 2 each time, so the next number is 9.

questions.prompt: A lamp is off. Which action is most likely to make it shine?
questions.explanation: Turning on the switch is the normal action that makes a lamp shine.

stageGroups.title: Spot the Simple Pattern
stageGroups.encouragement: Nice work. You are warming up quickly. Now the questions start asking for a little more attention.

questions.prompt: Which number is the odd one out?
questions.explanation: 21 is odd, while the other numbers are even.

questions.prompt: Which pair follows the same relationship as open and closed?
questions.explanation: Open and closed are opposites, and full and empty are also opposites.

questions.prompt: What comes next: left, right, left, right, ____?
questions.explanation: The pattern alternates left and right, so the next word is left.

questions.prompt: If every essay is a piece of writing, which statement must be true?
choices: Every piece of writing is an essay
questions.explanation: The statement directly says every essay belongs to the writing group.

questions.prompt: Which is the largest number?
questions.explanation: 0.9 is greater than 0.7, 0.62, and 0.45.

questions.prompt: A folder contains only blue sheets. You remove one sheet. What must it be?
questions.explanation: If the folder contains only blue sheets, any sheet removed from it must be blue.

questions.prompt: Which set is in the correct order from smallest to largest?
questions.explanation: 2, 3, 4 is the ascending order.

questions.prompt: If a seminar starts at 11:00 and lasts 45 minutes, when does it finish?
questions.explanation: Forty-five minutes after 11:00 is 11:45.

stageGroups.title: Everyday Logic
stageGroups.encouragement: That round tested common sense and clean reasoning. The next stage adds slightly more careful choices.

questions.prompt: Leo reads for 15 minutes, pauses for 5 minutes, then reads for 25 more minutes. How much reading time is that?
questions.explanation: Only the two reading periods count, so 15 plus 25 is 40 minutes.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion follows best: All borrowed books must be returned. This book was borrowed.
questions.explanation: If all borrowed books must be returned and this book was borrowed, it must be returned.

questions.prompt: Which choice is most like a syllabus?
questions.explanation: A syllabus is a plan or list of course topics.

questions.prompt: Which number completes the pattern: 6, 12, 18, 24, ____?
questions.explanation: The pattern increases by 6 each time, so the next number is 30.

questions.prompt: A file is thicker than a booklet, and the booklet is thicker than a sheet. Which is thickest?
questions.explanation: The file is thicker than the booklet, and the booklet is thicker than the sheet.

questions.prompt: Which instruction is most precise?
questions.explanation: It gives a specific task and a specific deadline.

questions.prompt: If one pass costs 5 units, how much do four passes cost?
questions.explanation: Four passes cost 4 times 5, which is 20.

questions.prompt: A researcher wants a stronger conclusion. What should they do?
questions.explanation: A conclusion is stronger when supported by relevant evidence.

stageGroups.title: Smart Choices
stageGroups.encouragement: You are now thinking like a problem-solver. Next comes a round of patterns and careful comparisons.

questions.prompt: A recipe for 3 people uses 6 cups of water. How many cups are needed for 6 people?
questions.explanation: Doubling the number of people doubles the water from 6 to 12 cups.

questions.prompt: Which sentence gives evidence rather than opinion?
questions.explanation: A measured rise in a score is evidence, not just personal opinion.

questions.prompt: Which item would be most useful for measuring time?
questions.explanation: A clock is designed to measure time.

questions.prompt: A box is wider than a case. The case is wider than a book. Which is narrowest?
questions.explanation: The book is narrower than the case, and the case is narrower than the box.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 2, 5, 9, 14, ____?
questions.explanation: The increases are 3, 4, and 5, so the next increase is 6, giving 20.

questions.prompt: Which question is best for testing cause and effect?
choices: What changes when the input changes?
questions.explanation: Asking what changes after an input changes tests cause and effect.

questions.prompt: Four scores are 6, 7, 7, and 8. What is the average score?
questions.explanation: The total is 28, and 28 divided by 4 is 7.

questions.prompt: Which plan is most likely to improve accuracy?
questions.explanation: Checking each step gives a chance to catch and correct mistakes.

stageGroups.title: Pattern Playground
stageGroups.encouragement: Halfway through, and you are still in the game. From here, the questions become more layered.

questions.prompt: Which number completes the sequence: 4, 8, 16, 32, ____?
questions.explanation: Each number is doubled, so 32 becomes 64.

questions.prompt: A code changes 3 to 12 and 5 to 20. What would it change 7 to?
questions.explanation: The rule is multiply by 4, so 7 becomes 28.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is strongest?
questions.explanation: Several tests give stronger support than preference, possibility, or no checking.

questions.prompt: If all rals are mips, and no mips are terns, what must be true?
questions.explanation: If rals are inside the mip group and mips cannot be terns, rals cannot be terns.

questions.prompt: Which set follows the same pattern as 4, 7, 10?
questions.explanation: 4, 7, 10 increases by 3 each time, and 5, 8, 11 does the same.

questions.prompt: A notice says: Register before Wednesday. Which action follows the notice?
questions.explanation: Tuesday is before Wednesday, so it follows the instruction.

questions.prompt: Which result would most weaken the claim: Extra practice improved the group?
choices: A similar group improved without extra practice
questions.explanation: If a similar group improved without the practice, the improvement may not be caused by practice.

questions.prompt: A square table has sides of 5 metres. What is the perimeter?
questions.explanation: A square has four equal sides, so 4 times 5 is 20 metres.

stageGroups.title: Applicant Thinking
stageGroups.encouragement: Now it feels more like an entrance challenge. Stay calm: every answer is still reachable with clear thinking.

questions.prompt: A student argues, 'This method is new, so it must be better.' What is the main flaw?
choices: The method has too many words
Newness alone does not prove quality
questions.explanation: Whether a method is new does not by itself show whether it is better.

questions.prompt: Which number best completes the pattern: 5, 11, 23, 47, ____?
questions.explanation: Each term doubles and adds 1: 5 to 11, 11 to 23, 23 to 47, so 47 becomes 95.

questions.prompt: A group has 36 people. One quarter choose history. How many choose history?
questions.explanation: One quarter of 36 is 9.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an assumption in this argument: 'The tutorial is useful because every seat is taken.'
choices: Every seat is taken because people value the tutorial
questions.explanation: The argument assumes the full room is due to usefulness, not some other reason.

questions.prompt: A scientist changes one condition at a time during a test. Why?
choices: To make causes easier to compare
questions.explanation: Changing one condition at a time helps identify what caused any change in results.

questions.prompt: Which is the best summary of: 'More evidence helps only when it is relevant and trustworthy'?
choices: Evidence quality matters as well as amount
questions.explanation: The sentence says both the amount and quality of evidence matter.

questions.prompt: If four machines make four parts in four minutes, how many parts do eight similar machines make in four minutes?
questions.explanation: Doubling the machines doubles the parts made in the same time, so they make 8 parts.

questions.prompt: Which choice shows the best lateral thinking?
choices: Ask whether the target has been defined correctly
questions.explanation: Lateral thinking often begins by questioning the framing of the task.

stageGroups.title: Evidence and Assumptions
stageGroups.encouragement: Good reasoning. The next stage pushes your ability to hold several ideas at once.

questions.prompt: A survey says 70% of 10 people preferred a service. What is the biggest caution?
choices: The sample may be too small
questions.explanation: A sample of 10 people is small, so the result may not generalise well.

questions.prompt: Which statement follows: If the bell rings, the session ends. The bell rings.
questions.explanation: The rule says the bell ringing causes the session to end, and the bell rings.

questions.prompt: What is the missing number: 3, 9, 27, 81, ____?
questions.explanation: Each number is multiplied by 3, so 81 becomes 243.

questions.prompt: A result is described as 'stable'. What does that most likely mean?
questions.explanation: A stable result stays similar across repeated checks.

questions.prompt: A map scale says 1 cm represents 4 km. How far is 6 cm on the map?
questions.explanation: Six centimetres represents 6 times 4 km, which is 24 km.

questions.prompt: Which question best tests whether a claim may be biased?
choices: Who benefits if the claim is accepted?
questions.explanation: A useful bias check asks whether someone has an interest in the claim being accepted.

questions.prompt: If no lecturers are applicants, and Nora is a lecturer, what must be true?
questions.explanation: If no lecturers are applicants, then a lecturer such as Nora cannot be an applicant.

questions.prompt: Which choice is most likely to improve a weak explanation?
choices: Add a clear cause with evidence
questions.explanation: A weak explanation is usually improved by a clear cause supported by evidence.

stageGroups.title: Two-Step Reasoning
stageGroups.encouragement: That was a strong round. Now you are entering the harder end of the challenge.

questions.prompt: A price rises by 20% and then falls by 20%. Compared with the original price, what is true?
questions.explanation: A 20% fall after a 20% rise is taken from the larger amount, leaving the result lower than the original.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion is best supported: Students who revised longer scored higher, but the study did not control for prior knowledge.
choices: Revision may be linked to scores, but other factors could matter
questions.explanation: The link may be real, but without controlling for prior knowledge, causation is uncertain.

questions.prompt: Which number completes the pattern: 2, 6, 12, 20, ____?
questions.explanation: The increases are 4, 6, and 8, so the next increase is 10, giving 30.

questions.prompt: An argument says, 'Most people in this seminar agree, so the claim is true.' What is the weakness?
choices: Agreement alone does not prove truth
questions.explanation: A claim is not proved true simply because many people agree with it.

questions.prompt: Four students each shake hands with every other student once. How many handshakes occur?
questions.explanation: The pairs are 1-2, 1-3, 1-4, 2-3, 2-4, and 3-4, making 6.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is the clearest definition?
choices: Evidence is when things sound true
Evidence is information that supports or challenges a claim
questions.explanation: It gives a specific meaning rather than a vague description or opinion.

questions.prompt: If all P are Q, and some Q are R, what must be true?
questions.explanation: The statement directly includes that some Q are R; nothing definite follows about P and R.

questions.prompt: A problem seems blocked because an unstated rule is being assumed. What is the best first move?
questions.explanation: Finding hidden assumptions can reveal a new way to solve the problem.

stageGroups.title: Oxford-Style Challenge
stageGroups.encouragement: Excellent. One final stage remains, and it rewards calm judgement over speed.

questions.prompt: A researcher finds that students who attend more tutorials also score higher. Which conclusion is safest?
choices: Tutorial attendance may be associated with higher scores
Tutorials are the only cause of higher scores
questions.explanation: The finding shows an association, but it does not prove a single cause.

questions.prompt: What is the next number: 3, 4, 6, 10, 18, ____?
questions.explanation: The increases are 1, 2, 4, and 8, so the next increase is 16, giving 34.

questions.prompt: A statement is necessary for an argument if the argument fails without it. Which is necessary here: 'The archive is reliable because it was checked yesterday.'
choices: The check could detect major errors
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the check being able to detect serious errors.

questions.prompt: A group of 6 numbers has an average of 11. What is their total?
questions.explanation: Average equals total divided by count, so the total is 11 times 6, which is 66.

questions.prompt: Which criticism is most relevant to a test with no comparison group?
choices: It is harder to know what would have happened otherwise
The title may be too short
questions.explanation: A comparison group helps show what happens without the tested condition.

questions.prompt: If every item in a set is either high or low, and an item is not high, what must be true?
choices: It is both high and low
questions.explanation: Within that set, anything not high must be low.

questions.prompt: Which phrase best describes a counterexample?
choices: A case that challenges a general claim
A longer version of a claim
questions.explanation: A counterexample is a case that shows a general claim may not always hold.

questions.prompt: A student solves a hard problem by first testing a simpler version. Why can that help?
choices: It can reveal the underlying structure
It removes the need for reasoning
It guarantees the answer is small
questions.explanation: A simpler version can make the structure of the harder problem easier to see.

questions.prompt: An applicant says, 'This explanation is stronger because it uses fewer unsupported assumptions.' Why might that matter?
choices: Fewer unsupported assumptions can make reasoning easier to test
Fewer assumptions make every answer correct
questions.explanation: Using fewer unsupported assumptions can make an explanation clearer and easier to challenge or verify.

questions.prompt: What number completes the pattern: 2, 6, 24, 120, 720, ____?
questions.explanation: The pattern multiplies by 3, then 4, then 5, then 6, so the next step is times 7, giving 5040.

questions.prompt: Which statement is logically equivalent to: If a result is verified, it has evidence?
choices: If a result has no evidence, it is not verified
If a result has evidence, it is verified
If a result is not verified, it has no evidence
If a result is popular, it is verified
questions.explanation: The equivalent contrapositive is: if it does not have evidence, it is not verified.

questions.prompt: A fair coin is flipped three times. What is the probability of getting three heads?
questions.explanation: There are 8 equally likely outcomes, and only one is HHH.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest objection to: 'No one objected, so everyone agreed'?
questions.explanation: Silence does not necessarily mean agreement.

questions.prompt: A theory explains the facts, but another theory explains the same facts with fewer unsupported claims. Which is usually preferable?
choices: The theory with fewer unsupported claims
The theory with the longer title
The theory that sounds more dramatic
questions.explanation: When explanations fit the facts equally well, fewer unsupported claims are usually preferred.

questions.prompt: Which answer best reflects intellectual humility?
choices: I may revise my view if stronger evidence appears
I am right because I spoke first
Changing my mind is always weakness
Evidence matters only when it agrees with me
questions.explanation: Intellectual humility means being willing to update a view when stronger evidence appears.

questions.prompt: A puzzle has many details, but only two affect the answer. What is the best strategy?
questions.explanation: Hard reasoning often requires identifying which details actually matter.
